results.possibleIdentity: Kent C. Holloway (1935–2004) was a San Francisco and Portland-based artist who gained recognition in the late 1960s and early 1970s for his innovative work combining art, light, and technology. He is best known for developing 'Sun Painting' (also referred to as 'Sun Screen') exhibits, which utilized sun-tracking mirrors to direct beams of sunlight through prisms, effectively 'painting' with light. Holloway frequently collaborated with physicist and artist Bob Miller, particularly at the Exploratorium in San Francisco, where they developed kinetic and light-based installations. His invention of a 'sun-tracking painting machine' highlights his engagement with mechanical engineering and artistic collaboration, aligning him with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). In addition to his technological art, Holloway was a symbolist painter and a figure in the San Francisco North Beach bohemian scene, often associated with Beat Generation figures such as poet ruth weiss and writer Philip Whalen.

results.possibleIdentity: Alan Raker was a professional baritone singer and opera director active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is most notably recognized for his role as the general director of the Asolo Opera Company (now known as the Sarasota Opera) in Sarasota, Florida, where he was associated with the Asolo Theater for at least 15 seasons as both a singer and director by 1974. His performance career dates back to at least 1949, when at the age of 19 he performed in Mozart's 'Don Giovanni' at the Nelson Gallery of Art in Kansas City. Throughout his career, he performed with various organizations, including the American Opera Society and the Philadelphia Grand Opera Company. Records from his youth at Easton Area High School in 1949 suggest he may have also pursued studies in mechanical engineering, which aligns with the multidisciplinary nature often associated with members of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, although a direct official record of his participation in E.A.T. projects was not confirmed in the search results.

results.possibleIdentity: Lewis MacAdams (1944–2020) was an American poet, journalist, filmmaker, and environmental activist who was a significant figure in the 1960s and 1970s avant-garde art and literature scenes. Born in San Angelo, Texas, he became a prominent member of the "Second Generation" New York School of poets, publishing works such as "City Money" (1966) and "The Poetry Room" (1970). In 1970, he moved to Bolinas, California, and later to Los Angeles, where he directed the Poetry Center at San Francisco State University from 1975 to 1978. MacAdams is best known for co-founding the Friends of the Los Angeles River (FoLAR) in 1985, an initiative he famously characterized as a "40-year art work" or "conceptual poem." His artistic approach often blended performance art with activism, such as his 1986 act of cutting a hole in the fence separating the city from the Los Angeles River. He was deeply embedded in the interdisciplinary circles of the late 1960s that included Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); he authored "Birth of the Cool: Beat, Bebop, and the American Avant-Garde" (2001), which documents the cultural history of the movements and figures associated with organizations like E.A.T., and he conducted high-profile interviews with many of its key figures, including John Cage.
